title: Props Basics - Sending Data Down
description: Learn how to pass data from parent to child components using props.
longDescription: Components become truly powerful when they can receive data from their parents. In this lesson, you'll discover how props work, learn to pass different types of values, and understand React's one-way data flow. By the end, your components will be dynamic and reusable!

learningObjectives.statement: Explain what props are and why they exist
Understand one-way data flow in React

missionNarrative.title: The Prop Delivery Service
missionNarrative.story: Welcome to the Props Peninsula! Here, data packages travel between component islands via magical bridges. As a new Prop Courier, you'll learn to package and deliver data to components throughout the realm. Ready to start your delivery route?

sections.title: What are Props?
Passing Props to Components
Important: Props are Read-Only!
Different Types of Props
sections.content:
## You Can Pass Any JavaScript Value

### Strings
```javascript
<Greeting name="Alice" />
```

### Numbers
```javascript
<Counter count={5} />
<Price amount={29.99} />
```

### Booleans
```javascript
<Button disabled={true} />
<Modal isOpen={false} />
```

### Objects
```javascript
const user = { name: "Alice", age: 25 };
<Profile user={user} />
```

### Arrays
```javascript
<TodoList items={["Buy milk", "Walk dog"]} />
```

### Functions
```javascript
<Button onClick={handleClick} />
<Form onSubmit={handleSubmit} />
```

sections.title: Multiple Props on One Component
One-Way Data Flow
sections.content:
React uses **one-way data flow** (also called "unidirectional data flow"):

```
Parent Component
     ↓ (passes props)
Child Component
     ↓ (passes props)
Grandchild Component
```

**Why is this good?**
- Easy to trace where data comes from
- Predictable behavior
- Easier to debug
- Clear component responsibilities

Data only flows **down**, never up or sideways!

sections.title: Using Expressions as Props

challenges.title: Identify Valid Props
challenges.description: Test your understanding of what can be passed as props
challenges.question: Which of these are valid ways to pass props?

options.explanation: Correct! String props don't need curly braces.
Correct! Objects can be passed inline with double braces.
Wrong! Unquoted text that's not a boolean causes an error.
Correct! Booleans work great as props.

challenges.successFeedback: Excellent! You understand how to pass different types of props!
challenges.failureFeedback: Remember: strings can be quoted, but other values need curly braces!

hints.text: String literals can use quotes. Other values need {}.
Objects can be passed inline: {{key: value}}

hints.finalExplanation: Valid props: strings with quotes, numbers/variables/objects/functions in curly braces, and booleans true/false.

challenges.title: Access Props Correctly
challenges.description: Choose the correct way to access props inside a component
challenges.question: How do you correctly access the 'name' prop?

options.explanation: Correct! Access props via the props object.
Wrong! 'name' alone isn't defined. Use props.name.
Nope! This is for class components, not functional components.
Also correct! This is destructuring - we'll cover it next lesson!

challenges.successFeedback: Great! You know how to access props!
challenges.failureFeedback: Use props.propertyName or destructure in the function parameters.

hints.text: Props are accessed through the 'props' parameter.

challenges.title: Complete the Component
challenges.description: Fill in the blanks to create a working component with props
challenges.question: Complete this Profile component
challenges.instructions: Fill in the blanks to make the component work correctly.
challenges.successFeedback: Perfect! You've mastered passing and accessing props!
challenges.failureFeedback: Check that prop names match between parent and child components.

hints.text: Prop names should be descriptive and match what you're passing.
Inside the component, access props with dot notation.
The parameter name in the function is typically 'props'.

challenges.title: Build a Product Card
challenges.description: Create a reusable product card component using props
challenges.question: Create a ProductCard component that accepts props
challenges.instructions: Create a ProductCard component that displays product name, price, and description passed as props.
challenges.successFeedback: Awesome! Your ProductCard component is ready to use!
challenges.failureFeedback: Make sure your component accepts props and displays all three properties.

hints.text: Define function ProductCard(props) with a return statement.
Wrap content in a div with className='product-card'.
Access each prop with props.propertyName inside curly braces.
Don't forget to display name, price, AND description.

reflectionQuestion.question: Think about a real-world scenario where you'd need to pass props. Describe a component you might build and what props it would need.
reflectionQuestion.sampleAnswer: I'd build a RecipeCard component that displays cooking recipes. It would need props like: title (string), cookTime (number), servings (number), ingredients (array), instructions (string), and imageUrl (string). This way I could reuse the same component for hundreds of different recipes!

resources.title: React Docs - Passing Props to a Component
